I substitute synthetic algae paste for real flour now that the trade routes to the agrarian moons have been severed by the secessionist fleets.
My oven is the only thing in this decaying spire that still provides warmth, as the central reactor flickers and the empire's heat dies.
I bake honey-cakes for the exiled nobility, who pay me in tarnished imperial credits that lose value with every passing hour.
The aroma of fresh sourdough is a fragile rebellion against the sterile, metallic scent of the atmospheric scrubbers failing around us.
I carefully ration the last bag of ancient heirloom yeast, knowing it is a more precious relic than any crown jewel stolen from the capital.
I knead the tension out of the boardroom air just as I would a sourdough, waiting for the deal to rise.
Much like a delicate soufflé, this acquisition could collapse if the CEO raises his voice even slightly.
I carefully glaze the final contract with flattering language to mask the bitter taste of the layoffs.
I treat the distribution of equity like measuring flour; a single gram of imbalance can ruin the entire batch.
I watch the shareholders simmer in their impatience, knowing exactly when to pull the deal from the heat.
I knead the wild seed paste with frantic strength, hoping the scent of baking hearth-bread will soothe the rage of the rival clan's warriors.
My oven is cold and silent because the fighting has driven the gatherers away from the calorie-rich grains we need for the winter loaves.
I watch from the smoke of my fire as the two chieftains scream across the valley, their anger as volatile as a poorly tempered dough.
The blood spilled upon the sacred earth has stained the very soil where I once harvested the golden seeds for my ritual cakes.
I offer a warm, honey-glazed biscuit to the wounded enemy, believing that the simple comfort of a baker's craft can bridge the divide of a generational feud.
I knead bioluminescent dough that pulses in rhythm with the city's central heartbeat, ensuring every loaf vibrates with the metropolis's current mood.
I carefully calibrate my thermal ovens to match the precise heat signatures of the geothermal vents venting from the city's subterranean processors.
I bake synthetic sourdough for the sentient skyscrapers, crafting massive, nutrient-dense crusts that help reinforce their living limestone foundations.
I sift stardust flour gathered from the atmospheric scrubbers, turning urban pollution into shimmering pastries that taste of ozone and electricity.
I negotiate my lease with the district's conscious zoning AI, pleading for more ventilation as my cinnamon rolls begin to influence the local weather patterns.
I have carefully kneaded the evidence into a cohesive narrative, ensuring no gaps remain for the prosecution to exploit.
My hands, dusted with flour and stained by the soot of the underworld's ovens, tremble as I face the gaze of the spectral judge.
I offer this loaf of honey-bread as a bribe to the jury of ghosts, hoping its sweetness might soften their eternal bitterness.
The courtroom air is thick with the scent of burnt sugar and sulfur, a combination that reminds me of my failures in the celestial kitchen.
I stand accused of tempering the souls of the damned with forbidden yeast, raising a rebellion from the depths of the dough.
I use the flickering heat of the emergency reactor core to keep my sourdough starter alive in this freezing abyss.
The rhythmic thrum of the leaking pressure valves sounds like the ticking of a kitchen timer in a house I no longer remember.
I sift through the rusted lockers of the hydroponics bay, hoping to find a stray bag of flour untouched by the salt-rot.
I knead the dough with a desperate intensity, trying to forget the oppressive weight of six miles of ocean pressing against the reinforced glass.
I shape the last of my rations into small, pale loaves, offering a scent of warm yeast to a crew that has long since stopped breathing.
I gaze at the minimalist white walls and wonder if these stark canvases could ever feel the warmth of a rising sourdough.
I examine the distorted sculptures and realize that the way I knead dough is its own form of tactile, visceral performance art.
I listen to the dissonant sound installation and find it mirrors the chaotic symphony of a commercial kitchen during the pre-dawn rush.
I contemplate the conceptual meaning of the empty plinth, seeing it as a void similar to the anticipation of an oven before the first loaf is slid inside.
I observe the crowd's bewildered expressions and recognize the same look of curiosity people have when I introduce a savory charcoal brioche.
I scrub my kneading table with vinegar and brine every hour, praying that the invisible miasma does not settle into the dough.
The line for my rye loaves has vanished, replaced by a ghostly silence and the distant, rhythmic tolling of the death bell.
I bake extra crusts for the physicians, though I fear the scent of warm wheat cannot mask the cloying stench of decay drifting through the streets.
My apprentice has developed the telltale swellings in his armpits, and I must now stoke the ovens alone while my heart breaks for the boy.
I trade my finest honey cakes to the city guards in exchange for a handful of clean salt and a promise that my shop will not be boarded up.
I tried to knead the sourdough, but my hands passed right through the dough as if the collision physics of the kitchen had simply ceased to exist.
I watched in horror as a dozen baguettes began to clip through the cooling rack, vibrating violently until they merged into a single, shimmering geometric mass.
When I checked the oven, I found that the timer was counting backward in symbols that don't belong to any human language, yet the bread was somehow already burnt.
I reached for the flour, but the texture of the bag had suddenly swapped with the floor, leaving me standing on a soft white powder while the tiles felt like paper.
I noticed a seam of static tearing through the air of my bakery, and for a moment, I could see the wireframe skeleton of the city stretching infinitely into a gray void.
